title: Quantum state texture of dynamical criticality
authors: Lucas C. Céleri, Krissia Zawadzki, Ivan Medina, Diogo O. Soares-Pinto
abstract: We investigate the role of quantum state texture in dynamical quantum phase transitions by establishing a direct connection between critical nonequilibrium dynamics and the recently introduced notion of rugosity, a measure of the quantum state texture. Considering a generic quench protocol, we analyze both standard formulations of the dynamical quantum phase transition. For type-I transitions, defined through the long-time behavior of an order parameter, we show that the time averaged rugosity, evaluated in the eigenbasis of the pre-quench Hamiltonian, acts itself as an order parameter, sharply distinguishing the dynamical phases. In the Lipkin-Meshkov-Glick model, this behavior is traced back to the underlying semiclassical structure, where the crossing of the excited-state quantum phase transition separatrix controls the redistribution of the state over the pre-quench energy basis. For type-II transitions, characterized by nonanalyticities in the Loschmidt rate function, we demonstrate that rugosity acquires a universal interpretation. For a suitable choice of basis, the rate function is exactly given by the density of rugosity, establishing a model-independent equivalence. Moreover, we show that even in physically motivated bases, such as the pre-quench energy eigenbasis, rugosity provides clear signatures of dynamical criticality. Our results place rugosity within a broader class of quantities diagnosing dynamical quantum phase transitions, including complexity and entropy production, while highlighting its distinct role as a measure of a basis-dependent quantum resource. This work provides an information-theoretic perspective on dynamical critical phenomena and opens new directions for exploring quantum texture in nonequilibrium many-body systems.
summary: This paper connects the concept of quantum state texture, called rugosity, to the identification of dynamical quantum phase transitions. It proves that rugosity can act as an order parameter for Type-I transitions and is directly related to the Loschmidt rate function in Type-II transitions. This establishes rugosity as a useful tool for diagnosing criticality in many-body systems.
main_problem: The study investigates whether 'rugosity' (a measure of quantum state texture) can serve as a fundamental diagnostic tool for identifying and characterizing both Type-I and Type-II dynamical quantum phase transitions (DQPTs).
main_result: The authors establish that for Type-II transitions, the Loschmidt rate function is equivalent to the density of rugosity in a specific basis, and for Type-I transitions, the time-averaged rugosity in the pre-quench basis acts as an order parameter.
method: The researchers use the resource theory of rugosity to analyze sudden-quench protocols, employing semiclassical analysis, finite-size scaling, and comparisons with other complexity measures like Krylov complexity and entropy production.
model_or_system: The primary model used is the Lipkin-Meshkov-Glick (LMG) model, a collective spin system undergoing a sudden quench of the transverse field.
key_observables: Rugosity (R_epsilon), Loschmidt rate function (lambda_t), magnetization (order parameter), and rugosity density.
important_parameters: Quench protocol (h0 to hf), transverse field strength (h), and the thermodynamic limit (N -> infinity).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a sudden-quench protocol and relies on the existence of a well-defined semiclassical limit and separatrix structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows time-averaged magnetization and rugosity vs. quench intensity; Figure 2 displays the derivative of average rugosity vs. quench parameter; Figure 3 compares the rate function and rugosity density.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of rugosity, applies it to Type-I and Type-II DQPTs using the LMG model, provides a mechanistic explanation via semiclassical structures, and contextualizes the findings within broader complexity and entropy theories.
why_interesting: It provides a new information-theoretic perspective on nonequilibrium dynamics, linking the generation of quantum resources (texture) to the emergence of thermodynamic phase transitions and complexity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Derivative of the time-averaged rugosity with respect to the quench parameter for the LMG model. This figure was constructed by computing the derivative of the data shown in the bottom panel of Fig. 1

FIG. 1. Order parameter for the DQPT (upper panel) and time-average rugosity (bottom panel) for the LMG model as function of the intensity of the quench. For all plots we set h0 = 0 and δ = 1, so the critical point is hf = 1/2. The time averaging was computed by choosing T ≈103ν−1, with ν being the smallest Bohr frequency of the energy eigenpectrum in both h and j considered range.

FIG. 3. Rate function and rugosity density for the LMG model. The upper panel shows the rate function λt, which characterizes the DQPT-II, while the bottom panel shows the rugosity density Rϵ/2j with respect to the pre-quench Hamil- tonian eigenbasis for the same values of the quench. The fluctuations observed in the higher peaks of both panels, es- pecially for the initial times, are due to numerical errors when computing the logarithm of very small numbers. For all plots we set j = 1000.
